questions.question: What mathematical problem does RSA's security rely on, and which problem underpins ElGamal and DSA?
options: RSA relies on the discrete-log problem; ElGamal/DSA rely on integer factoring
RSA relies on integer factoring; ElGamal/DSA rely on the discrete-log problem
Both RSA and ElGamal rely on the subset-sum (knapsack) problem
RSA relies on elliptic-curve discrete log; ElGamal relies on integer factoring
questions.explanation: RSA's security reduces to the difficulty of factoring large integers, while ElGamal and DSA are based on the hardness of computing discrete logarithms in Z_p*.

questions.question: According to NIST SP 800-57, roughly how many bits does an elliptic-curve key need to match the security of a 3072-bit RSA key?
options: 512 bits
1024 bits
256 bits
128 bits
questions.explanation: NIST SP 800-57 shows that 128-bit security requires ~3072-bit RSA/DH keys but only ~256-bit ECC keys, making ECC roughly 12× smaller for the same security level.

questions.question: In the Merkle-Hellman knapsack scheme, what property of the private sequence makes decryption efficient?
options: Each element is a prime number greater than all previous elements
Each element is larger than the sum of all preceding elements (superincreasing)
The sequence is sorted in ascending order with uniform spacing
Each element is a power of two, enabling binary decomposition
questions.explanation: The private sequence is superincreasing: each a_i > sum of all a_j for j < i. This lets the greedy algorithm recover the plaintext subset in O(n) time after converting the ciphertext to the superincreasing domain using the modular inverse of w.

questions.question: In ElGamal encryption, Bob sends (c1, c2) = (g^k mod p, M·y^k mod p). How does Alice decrypt using her private key x?
options: Compute c2 / c1^x mod p, using modinv(c1^x, p) to find M
Compute c1^x · c2 mod p to cancel the mask
Compute c2 - c1^x mod p to subtract the blinding factor
Compute g^x / c1 mod p and multiply by c2
questions.explanation: Alice computes the shared secret s = c1^x = g^(kx) mod p, then M = c2 · s^-1 mod p = M·y^k · (g^(kx))^-1 mod p = M, because y^k = g^(xk) = s.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the baby-step giant-step algorithm for solving the discrete-log problem g^x ≡ y (mod p), and why does this show that p must be large?
options: O(log p) — polynomial, so small p is enough
O(p) — linear, so p above 2048 bits is overkill
O(sqrt(p)) — sub-linear but still exponential in bit-length; p must be ≥ 2048 bits so sqrt(p) ≈ 2^1024 is infeasible
O(p^2) — quadratic, so even 512-bit p is secure enough
questions.explanation: Baby-step giant-step runs in O(sqrt(p)) group operations and O(sqrt(p)) space. For a 2048-bit prime, sqrt(p) ≈ 2^1024, which is computationally infeasible. Smaller p values (e.g., 512 bits) give only 2^256 security, now considered marginal.

questions.question: Why does elliptic-curve cryptography achieve the same security as RSA with much smaller keys?
options: ECC uses symmetric encryption internally, which is inherently faster
The best known attack on ECDLP (Pollard's rho) runs in O(sqrt(n)) group operations — fully exponential in key bits — while the best DLP/factoring attacks run in sub-exponential time
ECC keys are compressed using point-halving before storage, reducing apparent size
ECC operates over binary fields where addition is XOR, eliminating modular reduction
questions.explanation: For the elliptic-curve discrete-log problem (ECDLP) there is no known sub-exponential algorithm for general curves. The best attack, Pollard's rho, costs O(sqrt(n)) operations — exponential in half the key length. RSA and DH suffer sub-exponential index-calculus attacks, so RSA needs ~3072 bits where ECC needs only ~256 bits for 128-bit security.
